summary: Shaw argues that Riot Games violated California employment law by mining employees' in-game chat histories without their knowledge or consent, then using that data to identify employees for termination or reprimand. He details how the company analyzed past gameplay behavior, classified employees as toxic based on dubious sentiment analysis, and planned to extend this surveillance into their hiring process. Shaw enumerates eight specific legal and ethical violations, ranging from California Labor Code 980 to human research ethics requirements to discriminatory testing laws. He concludes that the practice deserves a class action lawsuit and criticizes the tech industry for celebrating corporate surveillance.
key_points: Riot mined employees' past in-game behavior without consent and used it to make firing decisions, likely violating California Labor Code 980
The study either violated human trials requirements by using real researchers or produced invalid results by using unqualified data scientists
The surveillance was applied asymmetrically — targeting employees who play the game for work while managers likely escaped scrutiny
Riot planned to extend this behavioral profiling into hiring decisions using a stoplight rating system in their applicant tracking system
Historical precedent with discriminatory employment tests like IQ tests suggests this kind of cultural behavioral test could be used to exclude people by gender or race
money_quotes: People were being identified based on either a flawed sentiment analysis of how snarky they were, or a manager's assessment of whether they were being snarky.

Either this study was done by amateurs who know nothing of sociology survey design and experimental design, or the study was done by trained Sociologists and those people have violated human trials requirements and should be held accountable.

An employer could have potentially recorded the audio of your home to figure out if they should fire you or not. Not even the government has that right so it's easy to say that no company should either.
Given enough time, Riot will find some dirt on any employee that they can use to fire anyone they like.
Programmers need to get their heads out of Ayn Rand's ass and stop worshipping corporations as some sort of infallible gods.
Corporations do not have your best interest at heart, and at best they're giving you a place to sit and do some job while they figure out how to fuck you over.
People will focus on the small percentage of toxic people and forget that Riot grabbed the chat logs of every person at the company to figure out who to fire without those employee's permission.
key_insight: When a company surveils employees' personal behavior without consent and uses it for employment decisions, it's not innovative HR — it's a legal and ethical violation that the tech industry shouldn't celebrate.
